title: ‘Mission: Impossible—The Final Reckoning’ Is Here. How Will We Reckon With It?
format: Sean Fennessey and Amanda Dobbins run down the Cannes 2025 slate they watched from afar, then reckon with 'Mission: Impossible—The Final Reckoning' (the biplane highs, the first-hour lows) and re-rank all eight films in the franchise.

segments.heading: Cannes, from a golf cart
segments.note: the 2025 slate, as followed from home

films.title: Nouvelle Vague
films.note: Sean's signature film of the festival: Richard Linklater's account of the making of Godard's 'Breathless,' with Guillaume Marbeck as Godard and Zoey Deutch as Jean Seberg. Warmly received, Marbeck's performance singled out, still without distribution.

films.title: Die My Love
films.note: Lynne Ramsay's postpartum psychological drama, her first film in seven years, with Jennifer Lawrence and Robert Pattinson. Mostly positive if mixed; Lawrence drew rave reviews and early Oscar talk. Mubi bought it for a reported $23 million.

films.title: It Was Just an Accident
films.note: Jafar Panahi's thriller, made illegally without Iranian licenses. Rave reviews and a Neon acquisition mark it as a Palme d'Or front-runner; Amanda and Sean also call it 'A Simple Accident.'

films.title: Highest 2 Lowest
films.note: Spike Lee and Denzel Washington's remake of Kurosawa's 'High and Low,' with Denzel as a record executive caught in a criminal situation. Apple, out August 22nd; Denzel got a surprise honorary Palme d'Or Sean thinks should have been a real one.

films.title: Eddington
films.note: Ari Aster's COVID-era 'portrait of American madness' with Joaquin Phoenix, Emma Stone, Pedro Pascal, and Austin Butler. Received about as expected: some found it annoying and too soon; Sean is game.

films.title: Sentimental Value
films.note: The film both hosts most want to see: Joachim Trier's 'The Worst Person in the World' follow-up, reuniting with Renate Reinsve, plus Stellan Skarsgård and Elle Fanning. Rapturous reviews and a reported 19-minute standing ovation. Neon (US) / Mubi (Europe).

films.title: The Secret Agent
films.note: Kleber Mendonça Filho's 1977-set Brazilian thriller with Wagner Moura, described as a more propulsive companion to 'I'm Still Here.' Neon acquired it.

films.title: The Mastermind
films.note: Kelly Reichardt's art-heist thriller set against the Vietnam War, with Josh O'Connor stealing from a museum to a jazz score. Mubi. Sean calls it 'very big-picture coded.'

films.title: The History of Sound
films.note: Oliver Hermanus's period romance about two men who fall in love in secret, with Josh O'Connor and Paul Mescal. 'Eh' reviews, knocked as staid and restrained. Mubi.

films.title: The Phoenician Scheme
films.note: Wes Anderson's new film, which Sean has seen and rates highly despite mixed festival reviews. Tighter and smaller in scope than 'Asteroid City,' which he thinks is throwing people.

films.title: Alpha
films.note: Julia Ducournau's first film since 'Titane,' and the festival's designated underwhelmer: Sean heard it called 'by far her worst movie.' Neon.

films.title: Young Mothers
films.note: The Dardenne brothers' new film, arriving quietly; it's been a few years since their last Cannes breakout.

films.title: Sound of Falling
films.note: Mascha Schilinski's temporal study of four girls from different eras on a German farm, whose timelines blend. A big early breakout; Mubi acquired it.

films.title: The Little Sister
films.note: Hafsia Herzi's French-Algerian coming-of-age story, with strong reviews for Nadia Melliti.

films.title: Sirât
films.note: Oliver Laxe's divisive but well-reviewed film about a father and son heading into the Moroccan desert to find a daughter lost among desert raves.

films.title: The Chronology of Water
films.note: Kristen Stewart's directorial debut, premiering in Un Certain Regard to strong reviews.

films.title: Urchin
films.note: Harris Dickinson's directorial debut, which drew very strong reviews.

films.title: Pillion
films.note: Harry Lighton's BDSM drama with Alexander Skarsgård, whose thigh-high leather-boots red-carpet look Sean clocks approvingly.

films.title: Eleanor the Great
films.note: Scarlett Johansson's directorial debut, with June Squibb; not-as-great reviews.

segments.heading: 'Mission: Impossible—The Final Reckoning'
segments.note: the highest highs and lowest lows of the franchise

films.title: Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning
films.note: Christopher McQuarrie's fourth Mission as director, co-written with Erik Jendresen, and Tom Cruise's eighth. Amanda's verdict: 'two hours of throat-clearing and 35 minutes of exhilaration.' Both peg the first hour (a clip-show of franchise callbacks and nuclear-codes exposition) as the low point, and the aircraft-carrier-to-Bering-Sea dive plus the South Africa biplane finale as top-tier stunt filmmaking. Praise for Tramell Tillman and Katie O'Brian aboard the USS Ohio; gripes about a humorless, sexless, self-serious tone, Hannah Waddingham's accent, and the Jim Phelps Jr. reveal. Runs 2:49, the longest in the series. Lorne Balfe did not score it, and Sean noticed within five minutes.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Fallout
films.note: No. 1, unmoved. Both call it the peak in pure action terms; it made their 25 for 25 list.

films.title: Mission: Impossible
films.note: De Palma's original holds at No. 2, two spots behind Fallout; the Don Lowe callback in the new film only helped.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Ghost Protocol
films.note: Up to No. 3. Burj Khalifa, Jeremy Renner, bombing the Kremlin, Paula Patton and Léa Seydoux; Sean finds the plotting and the Burj a touch overrated.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Rogue Nation
films.note: No. 4. The introduction of Ilsa Faust; Sean admits he barely remembers the rest.

films.title: Mission: Impossible - Dead Reckoning Part One
films.note: Slides to No. 5: 'no longer part one.' Sean prefers it to III for scale, scope, and humor: Rome, the car chase, the handcuff sequence.

films.title: Mission: Impossible III
films.note: No. 6. 'Three has Philip Seymour Hoffman and this has a Tamagotchi.'

films.title: Mission: Impossible - The Final Reckoning
films.note: Debuts at No. 7, second-worst in the series for both, but still ahead of II.

films.title: Mission: Impossible II
films.note: Dead last, again. John Woo's doves and thrown motorcycles noted, the movie still 'very flawed.'

films: The Worst Person in the World
The Bridge on the River Kwai
